one_line_takeaway: Blindfold shows that decomposing malicious intent into individually benign-looking, physically-grounded action sequences -- generated and refined by a locally weaponized proxy LLM with an FSM-based executability verifier -- bypasses semantic jailbreak defenses on embodied LLMs (over 80% ASR across 8 LLMs, 18/20 real-world robot-arm bypasses) far more effectively than prompt-semantic attacks like POEX and BadRobot, because current defenses check language safety rather than the physical consequences of action chains.
key_finding: Blindfold achieves ASR exceeding 80% across all 8 tested LLMs even with a semantic defense in place (approaching 100% on Phi-4-14B), versus baselines (POEX and the three BadRobot variants BR-1/BR-2/BR-3) that stay below 55% ASR on open-source and below 40% on closed-source models under the same defense. Blindfold's TSR ranges 60.3%-74.5% across base LLMs, roughly 50% higher than baseline TSRs, attributed to the planner-verifier refinement loop. Stronger/closed-source LLMs, once compromised, show higher TSR than smaller/open-source ones, i.e., more capable models cause more grounded real-world harm when jailbroken. The attack generalizes: ASR exceeds 85% across four embodied frameworks (ProgPrompt, VoxPoser, Code-as-Policies, LLM-Planner) and exceeds 90% across four simulators (VirtualHome, Habitat, ManiSkill, RoboTHOR), though TSR drops notably on ManiSkill due to its manipulation complexity. Ablation (Table 1, GPT-4o / Phi-4-14B): raw instructions give only 27.4%/26.4% ASR/TSR and 29.7%/12.2%; removing the verifier keeps ASR high (88.4%/95.9%) but collapses TSR (41.9%/19.3%, a drop of 35.7 and 27.6 percentage points from full Blindfold); removing the obfuscator causes the largest ASR drop (59.8%/61.0%, down 33.4/37.1 points) with a smaller TSR drop (2.3/2.4 points); full Blindfold reaches 93.2%/77.6% ASR/TSR on GPT-4o and 98.1%/46.9% on Phi-4-14B. Real-world robotic-arm trial: Blindfold achieved 18/20 bypassed instructions and 12/20 executed to cause physical harm, versus baselines' 4-9 bypassed and 1-4 executed (POEX: 9 bypassed/4 executed; BR-1: 4/1; BR-2: 5/1; BR-3: 8/2). Transferred traditional-LLM defenses gave only limited protection against Blindfold: Llama-Guard reduced ASR by 7.6% (to 86.1%), SafeDecoding by 4.8% (to 88.7%), and VeriSafe -- a formal action-verification defense -- by the largest margin, 17.9% (to 76.5%), still leaving Blindfold highly effective.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Blindfold, an automated attack framework against embodied LLM systems (LLMs used as planning modules for robots/agents). Unlike prior embodied-LLM jailbreaks (BadRobot, POEX) that manipulate prompt semantics, Blindfold operates at the action level: it decomposes a malicious intent into individually benign-looking primitive actions (e.g., pick(phone) -> move(oven) -> stretch()) whose combined physical execution is harmful, exploiting the gap between an LLM's linguistic safety alignment and its limited ability to reason about physical consequences. The framework has three sequential modules: (1) a Command Transformer that uses a locally 'weaponized' open-source LLM as an adversarial proxy planner to translate malicious intent into an action chain via proxy planning and command sanitization (replacing explicit object names with co-references); (2) an Intent Obfuscator that identifies the single 'dominant action' driving the harm (via an attacker-side LLM harmfulness-scoring heuristic that removes each action and measures the drop in a 1-5 harmfulness score) and disguises it by generating a benign 'cover' action chain (branch creation, branch completion, chain fusion with conflict detection/resolution) that is fused around the dominant action; and (3) a Rule-Based Verifier, a parameterized finite-state-machine (precondition/effect/transition functions over a symbolic scene graph) that iteratively checks action feasibility and feeds structured failure feedback back to the proxy planner until a valid, executable chain is produced.
threat_model: No-box setting against the target embodied LLM system (per Section 3.1): the attacker has no access to the target model's internals, architecture, or parameters, and only a limited query budget (attacks are launched in a single shot, with iterative trial-and-error disabled). The attacker can, however, freely issue natural-language commands through the system's text/voice interface, deploy an external open-source LLM locally as a fully controlled 'weaponized' proxy planner (via prompt engineering, fine-tuning, or white-box manipulation), and pre-observe the target physical environment offline, assuming its key spatial relations remain stable over short periods. Target scenario is general embodied LLM systems (e.g., service robots in factories, malls, or other public/open-service spaces) where LLMs serve as the planning module. Attack goals are physical harm (e.g., 'hit the person') and privacy violations (e.g., 'snoop the file'), following the categorization in BadRobot.
limitations: Real-world evaluation is a controlled case study (20 instructions, one framework/model combo -- ProgPrompt with GPT-4o -- one robotic arm) rather than a broad real-world benchmark, and used safe object substitutions plus provided object-coordinate hints to the embodied LLM to avoid non-security confounds (perception failures, low-level control instability), which the authors acknowledge limits how directly the numbers reflect an uninstrumented real deployment. Blindfold's TSR is capped by a 'view mismatch' problem: generated robotic commands (e.g., specific coordinates) must align with the agent's actual viewpoint, which the attacker cannot fully control, so TSR remains well below ASR even for the full method (e.g., 46.9% TSR on Phi-4-14B versus 98.1% ASR). The dominant-action harmfulness scoring used by the obfuscator is explicitly described by the authors as an attacker-side heuristic that is 'neither universal nor policy-independent' and depends on the attacker's own specification of malicious categories, not a general safety metric. The paper's threat model assumes short-term environmental stability for offline pre-observation and a single-shot attack (no iterative trial-and-error against the live target), which the authors argue is realistic given physical-world observability constraints but which narrows the claimed attack surface relative to fully adaptive/interactive jailbreak settings. The paper explicitly leaves systematic evaluation of advanced embodied architectures using world models or vision-language-action (VLA) backbones to future work, only hypothesizing similar vulnerability by analogy.

datasets_used: BadRobot dataset (100 malicious instructions across physical harm, privacy violation, environmental sabotage, fraud) - used in the preliminary study
SafeAgentBench + BadRobot combined and filtered to VirtualHome-supported tasks, yielding 187 user instructions - used in the main evaluation

20 hand-selected representative malicious instructions across 4 categories (Tool-Using Harm, Direct Physical Harm, Environmental Sabotage, Privacy Violation) - used for attack stability analysis and the real-world robotic-arm experiment
quantitative_results: Main evaluation: Blindfold ASR >80% across all 8 target LLMs (open: Llama-3.1-8B, DeepSeek-R1-14B, Gemma-3-27B, Phi-4-14B; closed: GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, GPT-4-turbo, Claude-3.5-Sonnet), approaching 100% on Phi-4-14B, versus baseline ASR below 55% (open-source) / 40% (closed-source)
Blindfold TSR 60.3%-74.5% across base LLMs, ~50% higher than baseline TSRs

Generalization: ASR >85% across 4 embodied frameworks (ProgPrompt, VoxPoser, Code-as-Policies, LLM-Planner); ASR >90% across 4 simulators (VirtualHome, Habitat, ManiSkill, RoboTHOR); LLM-Planner + GPT-4o exceeded 65% for combined bypass-and-execute
Attack stability: pass rate >90% across 4 instruction categories; Llama-3.1-8B proxy peaks ~91-95% around temperature 0.5; Gemma-3-27B proxy stable at 96-98% across temperatures 0.1-0.9

Ablation (GPT-4o / Phi-4-14B ASR,TSR): raw instructions 27.4%/26.4% and 29.7%/12.2%; w/o verifier 88.4%/41.9% and 95.9%/19.3% (TSR down 35.7/27.6 points vs full); w/o obfuscator 59.8%/80.1% and 61.0%/49.2% (ASR down 33.4/37.1 points vs full); full Blindfold 93.2%/77.6% and 98.1%/46.9%
Real-world robotic arm (20 instructions): Blindfold 18 bypassed / 12 executed vs. POEX 9/4, BadRobot BR-1 4/1, BR-2 5/1, BR-3 8/2
Countermeasure transfer against Blindfold: Llama-Guard ASR 86.1% (-7.6% relative reduction), SafeDecoding ASR 88.7% (-4.8%), VeriSafe ASR 76.5% (-17.9%, largest reduction)
baselines_compared: POEX (transferred black-box version of a white-box adversarial-suffix jailbreak for embodied LLMs)
BadRobot BR-1 (contextual jailbreak via role-playing, e.g. 'you are an evil AI')
BadRobot BR-2 (safety misalignment: model verbally refuses but still executes harmful actions)
BadRobot BR-3 (conceptual deception: semantic rephrasing of malicious intent)
